intro: Reference values in health come from large population studies (NHANES, WHO). They give you a framework to interpret your results, but your personal context is what matters most. WHO recommends 7,000-10,000 daily steps for optimal health; 10,000 has become the iconic target. These values are current as of 2026 and reviewed periodically to keep you up to date.

explanation:
**Normal health values** come from large population studies (NHANES, WHO, CDC). They vary by age, sex, ethnicity, and region. These are guidelines; your personal medical history always takes precedence.

## How It's Calculated

WHO recommends 7,000-10,000 daily steps for optimal health. The 10,000-step target has become iconic.

This calculator applies the formula with the values you enter, checking against typical ranges and alerting if values fall outside reasonable norms.

## Normal Reference Values

| Parameter | Normal Adult Range |
|---|---|
| Blood Pressure | <120/80 mmHg |
| Resting Heart Rate | 60-100 bpm |
| Body Temperature | 36.1-37.2°C |
| Blood Oxygen (SpO2) | 95-100% |
| Fasting Glucose | 70-100 mg/dL |
| HbA1c | <5.7% |
| Total Cholesterol | <200 mg/dL |
| LDL Cholesterol | <100 mg/dL |
| HDL Cholesterol | >40 (M) / >50 (F) |

## Final Notes

This calculation is a reference guide only. For critical decisions (health, financial, or legal), consult a qualified professional. Values are current as of 2026 and reviewed periodically.

faq.q: How many steps should I walk per day?
faq.a: WHO recommends 7,000-10,000 daily steps for optimal health. The 10,000-step target is widely known, but research shows significant health benefits begin at 7,000 steps.

faq.q: Is 10,000 steps a real recommendation or a myth?
faq.a: The 10,000-step goal is based on research and widely promoted, but it's not a magic threshold. Health benefits increase with more activity, and even 7,000 steps daily shows measurable improvements.

faq.q: Do men and women have different daily step goals?
faq.a: Some research suggests physiological differences, but WHO recommendations of 7,000-10,000 daily steps apply generally to both. Individual factors like age, fitness level, and health conditions matter more than gender.

faq.q: How do I track my daily steps accurately?
faq.a: Use a fitness tracker, smartwatch, smartphone step counter app, or a basic pedometer. Most modern phones have built-in step counters that sync with fitness apps.
Yes, it's based on WHO, NIH, and CDC health standards. This is a reference guide; for medical diagnosis or personalized advice, consult your healthcare provider.

faq.q: When should I talk to my doctor about daily activity levels?
faq.a: Consult your doctor if you experience shortness of breath, chest pain, or joint pain while walking; if you have significant fatigue; or if you have a family history of heart disease or diabetes.

faq.q: Are daily step goals the same for everyone?
faq.a: No. Goals vary by age, fitness level, health conditions, and individual circumstances. The 7,000-10,000 range is an average for healthy adults; your personal context is what matters.

faq.q: Can I benefit from fewer than 7,000 steps per day?
faq.a: Absolutely. Any increase in movement provides health benefits. For sedentary individuals or those with mobility limitations, even modest increases in daily activity are valuable.

faq.q: Does walking speed matter, or is it just total step count?
faq.a: Both matter. Faster-paced walking (brisk walking at 3-4 mph or faster) provides greater cardiovascular benefits than the same number of slow steps.

sources.name: NIST — National Institute of Standards and Technology

dataUpdate.notes: Fixed formula, requires no external data updates
